---
title: "Titanic Survival Analysis"
subtitle: "CIS 435 – Data Analytics Final Report"
author: 
  - "Radu Demian" 
  - "Dmitriy Zhemkov" 
  - "Jacob Foote"
  - "Lui Fuata"
  - "Greg Lampert"
  - "David Reese"
date: 05/05/2026
format: docx
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

The sinking of the RMS Titanic in April 1912 remains one of the most well-documented maritime disasters in history. Of the 2,224 passengers and crew aboard, more than 1,500 perished. The tragedy revealed stark disparities in survival outcomes tied to factors such as passenger class, sex, age, and fare.

This project was completed by a six-member team consisting of Radu Demian, Jacob Foote, Lui Fuata, Greg Lampert, David Reese, and Dmitriy Zhemkov (Team Leader). Collaboration was managed through GitHub and Discord, where the project repository and Quarto document were initialized.

The dataset selected is the Titanic passenger dataset sourced from Kaggle, containing records for 891 passengers across 12 variables including demographics, socioeconomic status, family composition, embarkation port, and survival status.

## Objectives

The specific objectives of this project are:

-   Understand the structure and characteristics of the Titanic dataset
-   Clean and prepare the data for analysis
-   Explore patterns and relationships through visualizations and statistical summaries
-   Engineer new features to improve predictive power
-   Build and evaluate multiple classification models
-   Compare model performance and identify the best predictor of survival
-   Translate findings into meaningful real-world insights

### Problem Statement
The central question driving this analysis is: What factors most influenced whether a passenger survived the Titanic disaster?

Specifically, this analysis addresses the following questions:

- How did passenger class affect survival?
- Did traveling with family improve survival compared to traveling alone?
- Did passengers who paid higher fares have better survival odds?
- Which combination of attributes best predicts survival?
- Can we build a model that predicts survival with meaningful accuracy?
# Dataset Overview

The dataset used in this analysis is the Titanic passenger dataset obtained from Kaggle. It contains 891 rows and 12 columns, where each row represents a single passenger.

## Variables

The following variables are included in the dataset:

| Variable | Type | Description |
|------------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| PassengerId | Numeric | Unique identifier for each passenger |
| Survived | Numeric | Survival status (0 = Died, 1 = Survived) |
| Pclass | Numeric | Passenger class (1 = First, 2 = Second, 3 = Third) |
| Name | Character | Full name of the passenger |
| Sex | Character | Sex of the passenger |
| Age | Numeric | Age in years |
| SibSp | Numeric | Number of siblings/spouses aboard |
| Parch | Numeric | Number of parents/children aboard |
| Ticket | Character | Ticket number |
| Fare | Numeric | Passenger fare paid |
| Cabin | Character | Cabin number |
| Embarked | Character | Port of embarkation (C = Cherbourg, Q = Queenstown, S = Southampton) |

## Data Quality

Initial inspection revealed the following data quality issues that were addressed during cleaning:

-   **Age** contained 177 missing values and was imputed using the median
-   **Cabin** contained 687 missing values — the deck letter was extracted where available and the rest labeled as Unknown
-   **Embarked** contained 2 missing values
-   No duplicate records were found in the dataset

# Methodology

## Week 1 — Data Preparation and Cleaning

The dataset contains passenger information from the RMS Titanic sinking, including variables 
such as survival status, passenger class, sex, age, and fare. There are several missing values 
in the age, cabin, and embarked columns, but no duplicate records were found.

We will use the data to analyze the factors that affected survival status among the passengers.

survived (0 = No, 1 = Yes)

Key Predictors: pclass, sex, age, sib_sp, parch, fare, embarked

There are a lot of missing values in this dataset. No duplicates.

The summary statistics show that age is spread across a wide range, indicating a diverse group 
of passengers. Fare is highly skewed, with most passengers paying lower amounts and a small 
number paying very high prices. The survival variable is imbalanced, more passengers died than 
survived. Because of the skewness in fare, it may need to be adjusted or capped for better analysis.

The age distribution appears slightly skewed. Fare is heavily right-skewed, confirming the 
presence of outliers. Most passengers traveled with few or no family members, as shown by the 
sib_sp and parch distributions. The survival count shows that more passengers died than survived. 
The majority of passengers were in 3rd class, and there were more males than females on board. 
Most passengers embarked from Southampton. These distributions suggest that class, sex, and fare 
may be important factors in survival.

The boxplots show that passengers who survived generally paid higher fares compared to those who 
did not survive. The fare variable contains extreme outliers, which can distort analysis.

After capping fare at the 99th percentile, the distribution becomes more stable while still 
keeping most of the data, making further analysis more reliable.

## Weeks 2–3 — Exploratory Data Analysis and Feature Engineering

The univariate analysis shows how each variable is distributed on its own. Age is somewhat 
normally distributed but slightly skewed, indicating a mix of younger and older passengers. 
Fare is heavily right-skewed, with most passengers paying low amounts and a few paying very 
high prices, which confirms the presence of outliers. The survival distribution shows that more 
passengers died than survived, so the outcome is imbalanced. Passenger class distribution shows 
that most people were in 3rd class, while fewer were in 1st and 2nd class. The sex distribution 
shows that there were more males than females on board.

The correlation analysis shows a negative relationship between passenger class and fare, meaning 
higher-class passengers paid more. There is also a negative relationship between passenger class 
and survival, indicating that higher-class passengers were more likely to survive. Most other 
relationships are weak, suggesting that survival is influenced by multiple variables rather than 
a single strong factor.

The scatter plots show that passengers who paid higher fares were more likely to survive. 
Lower-class passengers had lower survival rates, reinforcing the importance of socioeconomic 
status. Age does not show a strong or clear relationship with survival, other than the fact that 
if you were in a lower class you were more likely to survive if you were younger. Overall, fare 
and class appear to be more important predictors than age.

The group comparisons show clear differences in survival rates. Females had a much higher 
survival rate than males, and passengers in higher classes were more likely to survive than those 
in lower classes. Fare also varies significantly by class, with higher-class passengers paying 
more. These patterns suggest that both gender and socioeconomic status played a major role in 
determining survival.

Several new variables were created to better capture passenger characteristics. FamilySize 
represents the total number of family members aboard, while IsAlone indicates whether a passenger 
was traveling alone. Deck was extracted from the cabin information, even though a lot of values 
were missing and labeled as unknown. GroupSize shows how many passengers shared the same ticket, 
and FareBin categorizes fares into levels. The cutoffs at 8, 15, and 31 roughly separate 
low-cost tickets from mid-range, higher, and very expensive fares based on how the data is 
distributed. Since fare is heavily right-skewed, most values are concentrated at the lower end, 
so smaller intervals are used there, while a larger range captures the high-end outliers. These 
new features provide more structure to the data and may improve future analysis.

These visualizations help confirm that the engineered features and fare bins capture meaningful 
patterns in the data. The fare distribution with cutoffs shows that most values are concentrated 
in the lower ranges, which justifies the bin choices. The survival-by-bin plot shows clear 
differences across fare levels, supporting the idea that fare is linked to survival. Similarly, 
features like FamilySize, IsAlone, Deck, and GroupSize reveal patterns that are not obvious in 
the raw data, making them useful for further analysis.

## Week 4 — Baseline Modeling

Two baseline models were built: a linear regression model to predict fare, and a logistic 
regression model to predict survival. The dataset was split 70% training and 30% test.

## Week 5 — Advanced Modeling

The SVM model achieved 71.83% accuracy. It is better at predicting non-survivors 
(Sensitivity = 0.7857) than survivors (Specificity = 0.627). The confusion matrix shows that 
this model is better at predicting survivors and worse at predicting non-survivors.

Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN)

Specificity = TN / (TN + FP)
